metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` to the Part A.1 edit set — it imports `from rebar.llm import eval as _eval` (lines 408, 492) and `from rebar.llm import prompts as _prompts` (line 409) and must be updated (or re-exports added to `llm/__init__.py`) when those modules move to subpackages.
findings.finding: Part A.1 does not enumerate `src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py` as a consumer outside `llm/` that imports `from rebar.llm import eval as _eval` (lines 408, 492) and `from rebar.llm import prompts as _prompts` (line 409). Moving `eval.py` to `evals/` and `prompts.py` to `prompting/` breaks these two import sites unless `llm/__init__.py` re-exports them or the imports are updated in lockstep.
findings.location: Part A.1 — llm/ reorg; AC line: 'llm/ grouped into prompting/ + evals/; imports updated; suites green'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/fidelity_spot_eval.py` to the Part A.1 edit set: its `_RESULTS_PATH = 'eval_specs/...'` (resolved via `files('rebar.llm').joinpath(...)`) must be updated to reflect `eval_specs/`'s new location if it moves into `evals/`.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/fidelity_spot_eval.py` uses `files('rebar.llm').joinpath('eval_specs/fidelity_spot_eval_results.json')` (an `importlib.resources` anchor rooted at the `rebar.llm` package). If `eval_specs/` is moved into `evals/eval_specs/` as the plan implies ('fold in the existing eval_specs/'), this path string becomes stale and raises `FileNotFoundError` at runtime. This file is not in the plan's edit set.
findings.location: Part A.1 — llm/ reorg, evals/ subpackage: 'fold in the existing eval_specs/'
findings.suggested_fix: Either update `_RESULTS_PATH` to `'evals/eval_specs/fidelity_spot_eval_results.json'` and keep the `files('rebar.llm')` anchor, or change the anchor to `files('rebar.llm.evals').joinpath('eval_specs/fidelity_spot_eval_results.json')`.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before attempting Part A.2, run a pre-flight spike: `grep -rn 'spec_from_file_location\|rebar_reconciler\.' _engine/rebar_reconciler/` to produce an exhaustive list of all path-loader keys and sys.modules pre-seeding sites, and validate the lockstep update plan against that enumeration (or use this evidence to trigger the descope path).
findings.finding: The reconciler reorg (Part A.2) is flagged HIGH RISK due to ~15 sibling `spec_from_file_location` sites, sys.modules pre-seeding in tests, and Python 3.14 `__module__` constraints — but the plan includes no pre-flight enumeration spike to catalog all affected sites before committing to the lockstep reorg. The only mitigation is a post-hoc descope option after the risk proves unfavorable, which may come too late.
findings.location: Part A.2 — reconciler reorg (HIGH RISK — path-loaded, subprocess-isolated)
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit enumeration task at the top of Part A.2: run grep/AST scan to enumerate every `spec_from_file_location` call and every `sys.modules['rebar_reconciler.*']` pre-seed in tests; confirm the count matches the plan's '~15' estimate; only proceed with the reorg if the full set is confirmed and each site has a corresponding update action.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command (e.g. `grep -rn 'status=\|assignee=\|full=' src/rebar/_reads.py src/rebar/_engine_support/reads.py | wc -l` expected 0, or an AST-count assertion in the test suite) to the Part B AC that demonstrates the 14-param spread has been removed from the internal sites, not merely supplemented.
findings.finding: The AC 'a new filter touches 3 sites not 6' is a structural claim about code topology, but the plan names no proving command that verifies it — `make check` / suite green prove behavioral correctness but will pass even if TicketQuery is introduced yet old 14-param plumbing is left in place alongside it (both paths compile and tests pass). The structural property is unmeasured.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Part B AC line: 'a new filter touches 3 sites not 6'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Part B AC: 'Verified by `grep` (or equivalent AST check) that `_reads.list_tickets` and `list_states` no longer accept individual filter kwargs — only `TicketQuery` — and that `apply_ticket_filters` signature matches `TicketQuery`-derived fields.' This makes the structural claim machine-checkable at PR time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm explicitly in the Part A.1 edit set that `eval_specs/` is moved to `evals/eval_specs/` co-located with `eval.py`, so the `Path(__file__).resolve().parent / 'eval_specs'` resolution in `eval.py:66` remains valid.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/eval.py` resolves the packaged eval-spec directory as `Path(__file__).resolve().parent / 'eval_specs'` (line 66). If `eval.py` moves to `llm/evals/eval.py`, `__file__`'s parent becomes `llm/evals/` and the lookup for `eval_specs/` breaks unless `eval_specs/` is co-located at `llm/evals/eval_specs/`. The plan says to 'fold in the existing eval_specs/' but does not explicitly update this `__file__`-relative resolution or confirm the new co-location in the edit set.
findings.location: Part A.1 — llm/ reorg, evals/ subpackage; src/rebar/llm/eval.py line 66

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the actual count of sibling-load sites (raw `spec_from_file_location` + `lazy_load` calls) in `rebar_reconciler/` and explicitly account for `_loader.py`'s `_PACKAGE_DIR` in the reorg plan, since moving files into subdirectories invalidates its `Path(__file__).parent` anchor.
findings.finding: The plan states '~15 sibling-load sites duplicate this [path-loading pattern]' in the reconciler, but a `_loader.py` centralisation module already exists at `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_loader.py` that collapses the majority of those sites into `lazy_load(key, filename)`. The actual number of raw `spec_from_file_location` call sites remaining in the package is 30+, not ~15.
findings.location: Part A.2 — reconciler reorg, risk description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct site 2 from `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` to `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` (the file where the MCP `list_tickets` tool is actually defined at line 51 inside `register_read_tools`).
findings.finding: The plan names `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` as site 2 for the MCP `list_tickets` tool (the site whose signature must be KEPT), but `mcp_server.py` contains no `list_tickets` definition — it is a thin assembler. The actual MCP tool is defined at `src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py:51` inside `register_read_tools`.
findings.location: Part B — TicketQuery, site 2: `src/rebar/mcp_server.py` — MCP tool `list_tickets` (tool schema; KEEP signature)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`src/rebar/mcp_server.py` — MCP tool `list_tickets` (tool schema; KEEP signature)' with '`src/rebar/_mcp_reads.py` — MCP tool `list_tickets` defined in `register_read_tools` (tool schema; KEEP signature)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for the dataclass choice (e.g. 'dataclass is preferred over TypedDict because `to_engine_kwargs()` can encapsulate the None→"" coercions and `full`→`include_body` rename as a typed method, keeping that logic in one place').
findings.finding: The plan gives no positive rationale for choosing a `dataclass` over the structurally different alternatives available (e.g. a `TypedDict`, or a plain builder function returning a kwargs dict). The plan states WHAT `TicketQuery` does but not WHY dataclass is the right abstraction for this codebase — this is a missing positive rationale.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC1 to explicitly verify that `to_engine_kwargs()` correctly handles the `full`→`include_body` rename and None→"" coercion (e.g., 'to_engine_kwargs() correctly maps full→include_body and coerces None→""').
findings.finding: The described deliverable `to_engine_kwargs()` with its specific `full`→`include_body` rename and None→"" coercion responsibilities is not covered by any AC verification step; AC1 only checks threading and signature preservation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: '...`to_engine_kwargs()` correctly translates `full`→`include_body` and coerces `None`→`""` (verified by unit tests for TicketQuery.to_engine_kwargs).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the param count for `apply_ticket_filters` from '7-param subset' to 6 (`type_filter`, `status_filter`, `parent_filter`, `tag_filter`, `priority_filter`, `without_tag_filter`) and verify the `TicketQuery` dataclass is designed to cover all 6.
findings.finding: The plan asserts `apply_ticket_filters` is a '7-param subset', but the actual function in `reducer/_filters.py` has exactly 6 filter parameters: `type_filter`, `status_filter`, `parent_filter`, `tag_filter`, `priority_filter`, `without_tag_filter`.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the plan text to '6-param subset' and enumerate those 6 names explicitly in the `TicketQuery` field list.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit back-out plan for Part A.2: document the exact revert steps (e.g., restore flat layout, revert spec_from_file_location keys, revert sys.modules pre-seeding) so a partial reorg can be fully reversed, satisfying T4's reversibility requirement beyond mere risk acknowledgement.
findings.finding: Part A.2 reconciler reorg acknowledges extensive breakage risk (path-loader keys, sys.modules pre-seeding, Python 3.14 __module__ resolution) but provides no explicit rollback or back-out plan — only 'update in lockstep or descope'. The T4 criterion requires a reversibility mechanism, not just acknowledgement of risk.
findings.location: Part A.2 — reconciler reorg (HIGH RISK) section and AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' subsection under Part A.2: 'If the lockstep update fails mid-way, revert by: (1) git revert the Gerrit change for A.2, (2) restore the flat rebar_reconciler/ layout, (3) confirm subprocess isolation by re-running the reconciler integration tests.' Because the change lands as a single Gerrit change, a git revert is the natural back-out — make this explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE path for old flat `rebar_reconciler.<name>` sys.modules entries: specify whether tests explicitly delete stale keys in teardown/fixtures, and confirm no test leaves old flat keys registered after the subpackage keys are introduced.
findings.finding: The plan describes updating sys.modules pre-seeding in tests for the reconciler reorg, but does not define a RETIRE/cleanup path for the old flat-key entries (e.g., `rebar_reconciler.<name>`) after they are replaced by the new subpackage keys. Stale flat keys left in sys.modules across test runs can cause ghost-module hits and hard-to-diagnose test pollution.
findings.location: Part A.2 — reconciler reorg / AC line 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pytest fixture (autouse, session-scoped or module-scoped as appropriate) that removes legacy flat keys from sys.modules after the reorg, and document in the AC that the old key namespace is fully deregistered before the new subpackage keys are seeded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider whether Part B (TicketQuery) and Part A (dir reorg) should be tracked as two separate child tickets rather than sub-parts of one ticket, since they share no code, have distinct risk profiles, and are already planned as separate Gerrit changes — each is independently valuable, testable, and releasable.
findings.finding: The ticket bundles two independently-valuable, independently-releasable, and independently-testable concerns (TicketQuery logic refactor and directory reorganization) that share no code and have distinct reasons-to-change. Although the plan mandates separate Gerrit changes and ACs per part, the TICKET itself remains a single unit grouping two unrelated refactors.
findings.location: Context section: 'Two independent maintainability items… They share no code and land as SEPARATE Gerrit changes'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit AC or test-plan language covering: (a) tests calling `list_states`/`_reads.list_tickets` with the old `full=` kwarg are updated to use `include_body=`; (b) a unit test directly exercises `to_engine_kwargs()` for the None→"" coercion and the `full→include_body` rename; (c) any sys.modules-preseeding or patch() targets that reference the old internal signatures are updated.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a behavioral change at the internal `list_states` boundary (renaming the `full` kwarg to `include_body`) and a None→"" coercion inside `to_engine_kwargs()`, but does not mention updating or adding tests that call `_reads.list_tickets` / `_engine_support/reads.list_states` with the old kwarg name or with None values.
findings.location: Part B description: 'to_engine_kwargs() that owns the full→include_body rename and None→"" coercion'; AC: 'tests green'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Part B AC, add: 'Existing tests that call `list_states(full=…)` or `_reads.list_tickets(full=…)` are updated to use the new kwarg; a dedicated unit test for `TicketQuery.to_engine_kwargs()` covers None-coercion and the rename; no test patches the collapsed sites at the old signature.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `to_engine_kwargs()` (not raw `dataclasses.asdict`) is the mandatory mechanism at the two COLLAPSE sites (`_reads.list_tickets` → `list_states`), because those sites use `include_body` not `full`; reserve `**asdict()` or direct field access only for the 3 public boundary constructors.
findings.finding: The plan says TicketQuery will be threaded via '`**asdict` / a `to_engine_kwargs()`' without specifying which mechanism applies at each call site. At the `list_states` call site, `dataclasses.asdict(query)` would produce `{'full': ..., ...}` but `list_states` accepts `include_body` — so raw `**asdict` cannot be used there. Only `to_engine_kwargs()` (which owns the `full`→`include_body` rename and None→'' coercions) is correct at the internal sites. The phrasing leaves the implementer free to use `**asdict` at `_reads.list_tickets` → `list_states`, which would silently pass the wrong keyword and raise a TypeError.
findings.location: Part B — TicketQuery, description paragraph: '`**asdict` / a `to_engine_kwargs()` that owns the `full`→`include_body` rename'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '`**asdict` / a `to_engine_kwargs()`' with: 'a `to_engine_kwargs()` method (the sole mechanism at `_reads.list_tickets` and `list_states`) that owns the `full`→`include_body` rename and None→"" coercions; the 3 public boundaries construct the dataclass from their own typed params directly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and cite the Python 3.14 `__module__`/`sys.modules` key constraint for `@dataclass` resolution (e.g., reference PEP 749 or the CPython issue), and confirm whether it applies to currently-deployed Python versions, before using it as the basis for the A.2 risk/descope decision.
findings.finding: The plan's risk justification for Part A.2 rests on an unverified behavioral assertion about Python 3.14: 'Python 3.14 dataclass resolution requires each @dataclass module's `__module__` to match its sys.modules key.' If this assertion is incorrect or version-conditional, the stated risk profile (and any resulting descope decision) is mis-grounded.
findings.location: Part A.2 — reconciler reorg (HIGH RISK), paragraph beginning 'Python 3.14 dataclass resolution'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical citation: e.g., '(CPython gh-NNNNN / PEP 749 §X — only triggered under 3.14+ with deferred annotation evaluation)' and note which Python version the project currently targets so reviewers can assess whether the risk is present or future.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
